nativeRange.habitat: Sluggish blackwater tributaries, small rivers, and swampy areas with dense aquatic vegetation, submerged woody structures, and leaf litter; benthopelagic in shallow tropical freshwater

decorPreferences: dense planting with fine-leaved species (Java moss, hornwort)
floating plants to diffuse light and discourage jumping
driftwood branches and dried leaf litter for microbe colonies
gentle air-powered sponge filtration to maintain low flow

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates and zooplankton. In the aquarium, offer daily portions of small live and frozen foods: Artemia nauplii (baby brine shrimp), Moina, Cyclops, Daphnia, and grindal worms. Supplemental appropriately-sized micro-pellets and crushed flakes are accepted but live/frozen foods should form the bulk of the diet. Small upturned mouths require food that floats or sinks slowly; low surface flow keeps food accessible longer.

goodWith: small peaceful characids (ember tetras, neon tetras)
pygmy corydoras and other small callichthyid catfishes
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving large fish (outcompete for food)
cherry shrimp and nano shrimp (predation risk on juveniles)

summary: A rare, jewel-like blackwater pencilfish from the Capim River basin of Pará, Brazil. Reaches only ~3.5 cm and demands very soft, acidic water. Best kept in large shoals of 10+ in a dimly-lit blackwater biotope; displays a striking nocturnal color shift to vertical bars typical of the genus.
careNotes:
The Shining Pencilfish is a seldom-imported species that remains poorly known in the aquarium hobby, more often appearing as bycatch in shipments of other Amazonian fish than as a deliberately traded species. It is native to the Rio Capim basin in Pará state, Brazil — a blackwater drainage system — and demands the correspondingly soft, acidic conditions of its home waters (pH 4.0–7.0, very soft water, GH 1–10 dH). Hardness above roughly 10 dH and pH above 7.0 are associated with poor long-term health for blackwater pencilfish.

A minimum footprint of 60 × 30 cm is recommended. The tank should be heavily planted with fine-leaved and floating species, decorated with driftwood and a layer of dried leaf litter (catappa or oak leaves work well), and lit dimly. A dark substrate further reduces stress and encourages the fish to display its best colors. Filtration should be gentle — air-powered sponge filters are ideal — as this species inhabits near-still waters and strong currents are stressful.

Shoals of at least 6 individuals should be kept, though 10 or more is strongly preferred: larger groups reduce individual skittishness, suppress intra-group squabbling, and allow the fish to display natural social behavior. This species will not compete well at feeding time with boisterous or larger tankmates; tank-sharing species should be similarly sized and peaceful. A tight-fitting lid is advisable as pencilfish are capable jumpers when startled.

Like all Nannostomus species, N. nitidus undergoes a pronounced day/night color-phase shift: the daytime pattern of horizontal red-and-silver lateral stripes gives way to a cryptic pattern of oblique vertical bars at night. Research has demonstrated that this shift is autonomous — it continues even in blind specimens — rather than being driven by light perception alone.
breedingNotes: An egg-scattering free spawner with no parental care. Conditioning adults on varied live and frozen foods for several weeks is essential before attempting to spawn. Spawning takes place in a small, dimly-lit breeding tank filled with very soft (1–3 dGH), slightly acidic to neutral water. A mesh or grid base, or a dense mat of Java moss, allows eggs to fall out of adult reach. Spawning mops of fine synthetic fiber also work well. Eggs typically hatch within approximately 3 days, and newly free-swimming fry require infusoria or Paramecium as a first food; Artemia nauplii can be introduced once the fry are large enough. Adults should be removed immediately after spawning to prevent egg predation. Breeding is considered moderately challenging due to the strict water-quality requirements and the microscopic size of early fry foods.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nannostomus nitidus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water parameters (23–28°C, pH 4.0–7.0, hardness 18–143 ppm), minimum tank dimensions (60 × 30 cm), diet (micropredator; live and frozen small invertebrates), behavior (peaceful shoaling, nocturnal color phase), breeding method (egg-scatterer, ~3-day hatch, fry require Paramecium), and native habitat (Rio Capim, Pará, Brazil; sluggish blackwater tributaries with vegetation and leaf litter).

additional.name: FishBase — Nannostomus nitidus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Characiformes, Lebiasinidae, Pyrrhulininae), maximum size (3.5 cm SL), temperature range (24–28°C), native distribution (Capim River basin, Pará, Brazil), trophic level (3.2), conservation status (IUCN Least Concern), and occasional export for aquarium trade. Confirmed adipose-fin variability note in aquarium-bred specimens.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pencilfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Nannostomus care guide. Provided aquarium trade context: suitable diet (baby brine shrimp, Cyclops, Daphnia, crushed flakes, micro-foods), recommended temperature (24–27°C), compatibility guidance, need for tight-fitting lid, and low surface-flow feeding tips. No dedicated N. nitidus page; guide covers the genus broadly.

additional.name: iNaturalist — Shining Pencilfish (Nannostomus nitidus)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, common name confirmation (“Shining Pencilfish”), and distribution records within Brazil. Corroborated Pará, Brazil range.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scattering free spawner; no parental care. Requires very soft, slightly acidic water and dimly-lit breeding tank with mesh base or Java moss mat. Eggs hatch in ~3 days; fry initially require Paramecium or infusoria before graduating to Artemia nauplii. Remove adults immediately after spawning.
